title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “On the Label Trick: A Theoretical and Empirical Investigation”  
Rating: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary  
This paper offers a formal treatment of the so-called “label trick,” a heuristic in which ground-truth labels are injected into GNN training to improve predictions on unlabeled nodes. The authors provide a clear, formal description of the trick, develop a theoretical analysis of its regularization effects, and propose broader application scenarios within graph methods. Empirically, they evaluate the trick across several settings (Sec. 5.1–5.4; Tables 1–4) and report accuracy gains compared to baseline models.

Strengths  
1. Formal clarity: The manuscript articulates the label trick in precise mathematical terms, filling a gap in the current literature.  
2. Theoretical rigor: A sound analysis demonstrates how the trick acts as an implicit regularizer, offering insights into when and why it may help generalization.  
3. Broader vision: The paper successfully motivates how the label trick could be applied beyond standard node classification tasks, suggesting its use in various graph learning pipelines.

Weaknesses  
1. Empirical impact: Results in Tables 1–4 show only marginal accuracy improvements over strong baselines, and these gains fall within the reported standard deviations across ten independent runs.  
2. High variance: The large run-to-run variability undermines confidence in the claimed benefits of the label trick.  
3. Applicability to top models: The study does not demonstrate whether state-of-the-art GNN architectures—particularly those dominating the OGB leaderboard or the KDDCUP 2021 MAG240M-LSC challenge—actually gain from the trick.  
4. Limited metrics: Evaluation is confined to final accuracy; potential advantages such as faster convergence, enhanced robustness to hyperparameter choices, or training stability remain unexplored.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Ablation studies on top models: Compare identical high-performing GNN architectures with and without the label trick—especially those from the OGB top-10 and KDDCUP top-3 submissions—to directly assess its effect.  
2. Additional metrics: Report convergence rates (e.g., epochs to reach a given accuracy threshold), sensitivity to learning rate or dropout, and stability across random seeds.  
3. Motivation clarity: Elaborate on the practical desiderata (beyond accuracy) that have driven the community to adopt the label trick—such as faster training or robustness to noisy labels.  
4. Expanded experiments: Include real-world large-scale benchmarks from OGB and KDDCUP to validate the trick’s utility in competitive settings.

Overall Assessment  
This work excels in providing a formal and theoretical foundation for the label trick and hints at promising new use cases in graph learning. However, the current experiments fall short of convincingly demonstrating practical benefits. The reported accuracy gains are small relative to baseline variance, and key aspects—such as performance on leading challenge models and additional training metrics—are missing. I believe the paper makes a nontrivial contribution and, with the suggested additions, could serve as a definitive reference on this topic. In its present form, it sits just above the acceptance threshold. I recommend conditional acceptance, pending a stronger empirical validation of the trick’s claimed advantages.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper delivers a comprehensive theoretical treatment of the so-called “label trick,” which to date has largely been applied as an ad-hoc heuristic. The authors begin with the simplest learning scenario, derive the label trick rigorously as a regularization term, and then generalize their formulation to both regression and classification tasks. Building on this foundation, they propose several novel, theory-motivated use cases for the label trick and empirically demonstrate that these extensions yield promising performance gains. Overall, the work is well motivated, clearly presented, and substantiated by both mathematical derivations and experimental results.

Strengths  
1. Rigorous theoretical justification: The paper moves beyond heuristic arguments and provides a detailed derivation of the label trick, framing it formally as a regularizer.  
2. Generalized formulations: The authors successfully extend the regularization perspective to cover both regression and classification settings, complete with explicit derivations of the corresponding penalty terms.  
3. Clarity of exposition: The manuscript is written in a smooth, accessible style. Key insights are highlighted with insightful remarks, and the conclusions summarize the implications effectively.  
4. Novel, theory‐motivated use cases: Leveraging their theoretical analysis, the authors introduce new applications of the label trick that go beyond existing practices.  
5. Empirical validation: Experiments on representative tasks confirm that the proposed methods deliver tangible improvements, supporting the theoretical claims.

Weaknesses  
– The reviewer acknowledges having limited expertise in this specific topic area, which may constrain the depth of the technical critique.

Detailed Comments  
– Section 3 presents the core derivation of the label trick as a regularizer. The step-by-step development from a simple one-dimensional case to the full multivariate setting is thorough and convincing.  
– In Sections 4 and 5, the extension to regression and classification is handled with care: the paper clearly states the assumptions, derives the penalty terms, and discusses how they differ in each scenario.  
– The new use cases in Section 6 are well justified by the preceding theory and enrich the practical relevance of the label trick.  
– Empirical results in Section 7 align closely with theoretical expectations, and the performance gains are consistent across multiple datasets.

Recommendation  
I find this paper to be a strong contribution. It fills an important gap by elevating a common heuristic to a well-founded regularization framework, and it proposes valuable new applications supported by both theory and experiments. Despite my limited domain expertise, the clarity and completeness of the presentation leave little doubt as to the paper’s merit. I therefore recommend acceptance (rating: 8 – good paper).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper provides the first thorough theoretical analysis of the stochastic label trick in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and establishes a clear connection to classical label propagation. In particular, the authors identify and justify a simple yet effective fix—masking out diagonal terms in the propagation matrix—to prevent label leakage. They also derive an intuitive deterministic interpretation (Eq. 10) of the widely used stochastic heuristic. While the work addresses an important gap and offers valuable insights, several citation, clarity, and experimental details need to be addressed before the paper can be accepted.

Strengths  
1. The paper tackles a fundamental problem by formally linking classical label propagation to message-passing neural networks, shedding light on why the stochastic label trick works.  
2. The proposed off‐diagonal masking to remove identity mappings in the propagation matrix is well motivated and neatly resolves the label-leakage issue.  
3. This is the first work to unpack theoretically the now-common heuristic of injecting randomness into labels, providing mathematical grounding for a method used in top OGB submissions.  
4. Equation 10 offers a clear, deterministic view of the stochastic trick, making the core idea more interpretable for practitioners.

Weaknesses  
1. The manuscript fails to credit a prior work (arXiv:2009.03509) that originally introduced the stochastic label trick.  
2. There is little intuition behind Equation (3) regarding why training multiplies Y_tr by a weight matrix W, nor for the trainable smoothing step in the Correct & Smooth framework.  
3. The authors omit comparisons to related methods that combine label and feature propagation, notably the generative framework of Jia & Benson (2021).  
4. Several derivations are too terse: the transition from the penultimate to the final form of Equation (5) lacks intermediate steps, and the expectation subscript “_splits_” is never clearly defined.  
5. Section 4.1, which proposes learning weights for the Neumann-series-based propagation, is vague on the actual training procedure and how the final model relates to standard adjacency-learning methods.  
6. Experimental details are incomplete: Table 2 reports regression metrics but omits R² scores; Figure 1 does not specify which GNN backbone was used nor include a deterministic-label-trick baseline; and Table 3 does not clarify whether its results use the regularized deterministic form.  
7. The paper contains several typos and inconsistent formatting (e.g., “insure” vs. “ensure,” mismatched brackets), which detract from readability.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Acknowledge and cite the earlier label trick paper (arXiv:2009.03509) when introducing the stochastic approach.  
2. Add intuitive discussion for Equation (3): explain why and how post-multiplying Y_tr by W captures trainable smoothing.  
3. Include a comparison with Jia & Benson’s 2021 generative label‐feature propagation framework to contextualize your contribution.  
4. Clarify the notation around E_splits—define the random splits and their role in expectations—and show the intermediate algebraic steps leading to the final form of Eq. (5).  
5. Rewrite dense derivations in a more stepwise fashion and simplify notation where possible to aid comprehension.  
6. In Section 4.1, detail how the Neumann-series weights are learned, the optimization objective, and the connection to existing adjacency-learning or regularization techniques.  
7. For Table 2 regression results, report R² alongside current metrics.  
8. In Figure 1 (right), specify the GNN architecture used and add results for the deterministic label-trick baseline for direct comparison.  
9. In Remark 4, expand the discussion on overfitting when regularization is zero and provide empirical or theoretical justification.  
10. State explicitly whether the deterministic regularized form was used in the Table 3 experiments; if not, explain the rationale.  
11. Conduct a thorough proofread to fix typographical errors and ensure consistent bracket usage throughout.

Conclusion  
This work makes a valuable theoretical contribution by demystifying a popular stochastic labeling heuristic in GNNs and connecting it to classical propagation methods. However, due to missing prior citations, opaque derivations, incomplete comparisons to related frameworks, and insufficient experimental details, the paper in its current form falls short of the bar for acceptance. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer appreciates the paper’s theoretical analysis of the stochastic label trick in GNNs and its connection to classical label propagation, but raises concerns about missing citations, unclear derivations and notation, and insufficient detail in certain sections.
strengths: Addresses a fundamental problem linking classical label propagation and message-passing neural networks
Well-motivated solution to avoid identity mapping by removing diagonal terms in the propagation matrix
Provides the first careful theoretical unpacking of the widely used heuristic label trick
Equation (10) offers an intuitive deterministic interpretation of the stochastic label trick
weaknesses: Fails to credit an earlier work (arXiv:2009.03509) that first proposed the label trick
Lacks intuition behind Equation (3) and the trainable smoothing step in Correct & Smooth
Omits comparison to related work on combining label and feature propagation (e.g., Jia & Benson 2021)
Several equations are unclear, notation is dense, and the expectation subscript “_splits_” is hard to parse
Derivation from the penultimate to final form of Equation (5) is not sufficiently explained
Section 4.1 is vague about training procedure and final model form
Typographical and formatting inconsistencies (e.g., “insure” vs. “ensure,” bracket usage)
suggestions: Acknowledge and cite earlier label trick work (arXiv:2009.03509)
Provide intuitive motivation for post-multiplying Y_tr by a weight matrix in Equation (3)
Include a comparison to Jia & Benson’s generative framework unifying label propagation and GNNs
Clarify the subscript and meaning of E_splits, and add steps for the transition in Equation (5)
Rewrite dense sections and reduce notational complexity for better readability
Expand Section 4.1 with details on how the trainable label propagation model is trained and how it relates to Neumann series or existing adjacency-learning methods
Use R² as the evaluation metric for regression tasks in Table 2
Specify which GNN is used in Figure 1 and include the deterministic label trick baseline
Explain the overfitting claim in Remark 4 when regularization is zero
State whether the deterministic regularized form was used in Table 3 experiments, and if not, why
Fix typos and ensure consistent use of parentheses vs. brackets
main_points: Paper tackles an important problem by theoretically analyzing the stochastic label trick in GNNs
The off-diagonal masking in the propagation matrix is a simple yet effective fix to label leakage
The work justifies a heuristic method long used in top OGB submissions
Missing attribution to prior work on the label trick
Notation density and unclear derivations impede comprehension
Lack of explicit comparisons to related methods and theoretical frameworks
Insufficient practical detail in novel application sections
technical_details: Equation (3): unclear why Y_tr is post-multiplied by a weight matrix W
Subscript usage and meaning of E_splits not well defined
Derivation from the second-last to last term in Equation (5) needs elaboration
Section 4.1’s proposal to learn weights for the Neumann series lacks mathematical detail
Table 2: regression results should report R²
Figure 1 (right): the choice of GNN model is not specified and deterministic baseline is omitted

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

reviews.text:
Review for Paper “Deterministic Label Trick for GNNs”

Summary  
This paper proposes a deterministic alternative to the commonly used stochastic label trick in graph neural networks. The authors introduce a self-exclusion operator that prevents label leakage without random sampling and prove in Theorem 2 an upper bound on the cross-entropy loss under this deterministic scheme. While the theoretical contribution is interesting, the paper’s motivation for replacing the stochastic method remains unconvincing, and the empirical evaluation does not clearly demonstrate a practical benefit. Overall, I find the work marginally below the acceptance threshold (score 5).

Strengths  
1. The paper presents a novel deterministic framework—via a self-exclusion operator—that avoids randomness in label propagation, which to my knowledge has not been studied in this way before.  
2. Theorem 2 is a valuable theoretical result: it establishes an upper bound on the cross-entropy loss for the proposed deterministic approach, giving insight into its theoretical behavior.

Weaknesses  
1. Motivation and Practical Benefit: Beyond the desire to eliminate randomness, the paper does not convincingly argue why a deterministic approach is preferable. There is no analysis of potential speedups, variance reduction, or other concrete computational or accuracy advantages over stochastic sampling.  
2. Empirical Validation: The experiments lack direct comparisons against leading stochastic label-trick methods. Without head-to-head results, it is unclear whether the deterministic trick offers any real-world improvement.  
3. Statistical Significance: Reported gains in accuracy are often marginal and within the range of standard deviations, raising questions about their reliability.  
4. Missing Related Work: The manuscript omits key literature, notably Szlam, Maggioni & Coifman (2008) on function-adapted diffusion processes for regularization on graphs. This work is highly relevant to label propagation and diffusion-based methods.  
5. Incomplete Method Descriptions: External algorithms such as the Correct & Smooth (C&S) method are referenced without a self-contained description or a clear citation, making it hard for readers to reproduce or contextualize the comparisons.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify Motivation: Articulate clear practical benefits of determinism—e.g., reduced runtime variability, reproducibility, or theoretical guarantees—that go beyond simply avoiding randomness.  
2. Direct Comparisons: Include experiments that pit the proposed deterministic trick against state-of-the-art stochastic label tricks under the same settings.  
3. Statistical Analysis: Report confidence intervals or conduct significance tests to demonstrate that observed improvements are meaningful.  
4. Enrich Related Work: Add and discuss Szlam, Maggioni & Coifman (2008) on function-adapted diffusion and any other relevant label propagation methods.  
5. Self-Containment: Provide concise descriptions (or pseudocode) and proper citations for all referenced algorithms, including C&S, so readers can fully understand and reproduce your evaluation.

Conclusion  
The paper’s core idea of a deterministic self-exclusion operator is novel and the theoretical bound in Theorem 2 is sound. However, the unclear practical motivation, lack of direct empirical comparisons to strong baselines, marginal gains relative to variability, and omission of key related work weaken its overall impact. I therefore recommend marginal rejection (score 5), with encouragement to address the above points for a stronger resubmission.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents an insightful theoretical framework showing that, when the expert policy is deterministic, imitation learning (IL) can be cast as a standard reinforcement learning (RL) problem with a stationary reward. The authors prove that this stationary reward formulation is equivalent to minimizing the total variation distance between the expert’s and the learner’s state–action distributions. Their proofs are clear and accessible, and the experiments explore how the quantity of expert data affects IL performance.

Strengths  
1. Theoretical Contribution: The reduction of imitation learning for deterministic experts to an RL problem with a fixed reward is novel and well justified.  
2. Connection to f-Divergences: Establishing that the stationary reward exactly minimizes total variation provides a clean link to adversarial IL methods.  
3. Clarity of Proofs: All derivations are well motivated and easy to follow.  
4. Empirical Investigation: The experiments systematically vary the amount of expert data, illuminating how data scarcity impacts IL outcomes.

Weaknesses  
1. Soft Reward vs. Deterministic Assumption: In practice, the authors use a soft reward R′(s,a) = 1 – min_{(s′,a′)∈D} ‖(s,a)–(s′,a′)‖₂, which violates the core assumption of a truly deterministic expert policy that underpins the theory.  
2. Narrow Empirical Scope: The expert policies are trained with SAC (which constrains actions internally) rather than the stronger Gaussian actors with clipping used in prior IL benchmarks, raising questions about how representative these results are.  
3. Unexplored Training Details: The impact of different expert training procedures—such as action constraints versus post-hoc clipping—on imitation performance is not studied, making it difficult to generalize the findings.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical Clarification: Provide an analysis of how using the soft L₂-based reward relaxation affects the theoretical guarantees that rely on deterministic experts.  
2. Broader Experiments: Augment the empirical evaluation with stronger expert policies drawn from established benchmarks (e.g., Gaussian policies with clipping) to assess whether the results hold under more challenging settings.  
3. Training Procedure Analysis: Investigate how variations in expert training (e.g., action space constraints versus clipping) influence the quality of imitation.  
4. Comparative Study: Directly compare the proposed soft reward formulation to the binary-support reward design from Wang et al. (2019) to highlight trade-offs.  
5. Practical Assumptions: Discuss how a practitioner might verify or enforce the deterministic-expert assumption in real-world data, and how to distinguish deterministic from stochastic experts given limited samples.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, the paper offers a compelling new theoretical lens on imitation learning and supports it with a set of clear proofs and preliminary experiments. While the practical adaptation to soft rewards and the limited empirical scope leave important open questions, these do not overshadow the conceptual advance. I therefore recommend marginal acceptance (Rating: 6/10).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.

reviews.text:
Review for “Deterministic-Expert Imitation Learning via Stationary Rewards”

Summary  
This paper introduces a novel theoretical reduction that casts deterministic-expert imitation learning as a reinforcement learning (RL) problem with a stationary (average) reward function. The authors prove that under this formulation one can exactly recover the expert’s reward and bound the total variation distance between the expert’s and the learner’s policies. Empirical results on benchmark tasks demonstrate that, in practice, applying off-the-shelf RL algorithms to the constructed stationary-reward MDP achieves performance competitive with standard imitation-learning baselines.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of Reduction: The paper provides a fresh perspective by reducing deterministic-expert imitation learning to an average-reward RL problem, a direction not widely explored.  
2. Theoretical Guarantees: The authors derive formal results showing both exact expert-reward recovery and quantitative bounds on the learner’s total variation distance to the expert policy.  
3. Empirical Validation: Experiments on several control and navigation tasks confirm that the proposed reduction yields a practical algorithm with performance on par with existing imitation methods.

Weaknesses  
Despite its appealing ideas, the paper suffers from several clarity and presentation issues that hinder its accessibility and reproducibility:  
1. Average-Reward Formulation Motivation: The choice of an average-reward MDP, as opposed to the more common discounted setting, lacks motivation or intuition.  
2. Undefined Notation: The notation for the total variation norm, ‖·‖_{TV}, is used in the main theorems without a formal definition or reference.  
3. Ambiguous Algorithmic Scope: Proposition 1 guarantees require an “RL solver” with certain properties, but it is unclear which existing algorithms satisfy these requirements.  
4. Missing Reward Definitions: The manuscript introduces intrinsic reward R_int and extrinsic reward R_ext without formally defining them or contrasting their roles.  
5. Learning Mechanism Details: Section 3 outlines how the learner uses the intrinsic reward signal to match the expert policy, yet the algorithmic steps and learning dynamics are not described in sufficient detail.  
6. Experimental Protocol Gaps: The description of comparative experiments omits key details such as hyperparameter choices, evaluation metrics, and statistical reporting necessary for reproducibility.  
7. Organization and Background: The overall flow of the paper, particularly the background on average vs. discounted MDPs and imitation-learning preliminaries, is sparse, making it challenging for readers to follow the technical development.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Average vs. Discounted Reward: Add a dedicated discussion—perhaps in the introduction or background section—explaining why the average-reward formulation was chosen and under what circumstances it is preferred over discounted MDPs.  
2. Define Total Variation Norm: Include a concise definition of the total variation distance ‖μ−ν‖_{TV} (or a reference) when it first appears, so readers can precisely interpret the theoretical bounds.  
3. Specify Compatible RL Solvers: In the theoretical section, list or give examples of RL algorithms (e.g., average-reward actor–critic, relative value iteration) that meet the assumptions of Proposition 1, or at least clarify the properties required of the solver.  
4. Formalize R_int and R_ext: Introduce formal definitions for intrinsic and extrinsic reward functions, accompanied by intuition or examples that illustrate their differences and roles in the reduction.  
5. Elaborate Section 3 Algorithmically: Provide a step-by-step algorithm or pseudocode showing how the learner computes and optimizes the intrinsic reward to recover the expert policy, including any learning rates, update rules, or convergence criteria.  
6. Detail Experimental Setup: Expand the experiments section to describe the evaluation protocol fully: specify hyperparameters, the number of random seeds, performance metrics, and statistical significance measures used in comparisons.  
7. Enhance Structure and Background: Reorganize the paper to include a more thorough background on imitation learning and MDP formulations. Consider adding a roadmap paragraph at the end of the introduction and moving some of the technical preliminaries to a dedicated section.

Overall Assessment  
This work tackles an interesting and theoretically grounded approach to imitation learning, backed by empirical results that showcase its promise. However, to reach its full potential and to be accessible to the broader community, the paper requires substantial improvements in clarity, definitional rigor, and experimental detail. I recommend a marginal acceptance (Rating: 6), contingent on addressing the issues outlined above.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.

Main Review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel reduction from imitation learning to a single call of reinforcement learning (RL) by defining an intrinsic stationary sparse reward: it assigns a reward of 1 whenever the learner’s state–action pair matches an expert demonstration, and 0 otherwise. The authors provide a clear and concise formulation, a rigorous theoretical analysis establishing high-probability performance guarantees, and empirical experiments that align with their theoretical claims. Overall, the paper is well written, and its core idea of leveraging a single RL invocation for imitation is both elegant and compelling.

Strengths  
1. Simplicity of Implementation: Compared to many existing imitation learning methods, the proposed approach is straightforward to execute. Constructing the intrinsic reward from expert data requires only a simple matching condition, avoiding complex adversarial or density-estimation machinery.  
2. Clarity and Presentation: The writing is crisp and the exposition is very clear. Definitions, assumptions, and theorem statements are presented in a way that is easy to follow.  
3. Rigorous Theory: The authors derive strong, high-probability bounds on the performance gap between the learned and expert policies. This theoretical contribution is solid and deepens our understanding of imitation via sparse rewards.  
4. Novel Reduction: Reducing imitation learning to a single RL optimization is novel and potentially impactful, as it unifies these two strands of research under a simple framework.  
5. Empirical Support: The experiments are aligned with the theoretical predictions, demonstrating that the method can recover expertlike behavior in benchmark tasks.

Weaknesses  
1. Extreme Reward Sparsity: By construction, the intrinsic reward is zero almost everywhere except at exact expert state–action pairs. Such sparsity is well known to impede RL exploration and learning, raising concerns about practical trainability.  
2. Convergence Speed: The paper does not address how the extreme reward sparsity might slow down convergence in realistic settings. In the absence of richer shaping or auxiliary objectives, standard RL solvers may struggle to find the sparse “signal.”  
3. Lack of Runtime Comparisons: There is no evaluation of wall‐clock training time or iteration‐to‐convergence across the baselines (ILR, GAIL, SQIL, etc.). Consequently, it is difficult to gauge whether the proposed method offers any real computational advantage.  
4. Practical Applicability: While the theoretical guarantees are strong, the paper does not discuss strategies for mitigating the sparse‐reward challenge in practice. This omission leaves open the question of how the approach would fare on more complex or high‐dimensional tasks.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Mitigation Strategies: Please include a discussion (or preliminary experiments) on potential techniques to alleviate sparse-reward issues. For instance, could one incorporate simple reward shaping, auxiliary exploration bonuses, or curriculum learning to guide RL toward the expert demonstrations more efficiently?  
2. Convergence and Runtime Metrics: Augment the empirical section with computation‐time or convergence‐rate plots comparing the proposed method against GAIL, SQIL, and other relevant baselines. Reporting the number of environment interactions or wall‐clock hours needed to reach a given performance threshold would clarify the method’s practical viability.

Overall Recommendation (Rating: 6 – marginally above threshold)  
I find the paper’s core idea—reducing imitation learning to a single RL call with a stationary sparse reward—to be novel and theoretically well justified. The clear writing, simple implementation, and rigorous analysis are significant positives. However, the practicality of training under such extreme reward sparsity remains uncertain without further discussion or empirical evidence. Addressing the above suggestions would strengthen the submission. Given the paper’s theoretical contributions and elegance, I recommend a marginal acceptance.
