reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes a new randomized exploration method termed HyperDQN. 

It aims to improve the popular Randomized Least Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) method by addressing RLSVI's major limitations of computational burden and its requirement of known good features in advance to adopt the linear setting. 

The principal of HyperDQN is to combine DQN with a probabilistic hypermodel which could generate posterior samples for the parameter values of the weights at the last linear layer of the Q-function. Both the hypermodel and the Q-function could be jointly optimized during training. By sampling the Q-function parameters, it helps the model to select exploratory action sequences.

 Empirical evaluation results are provided in Atari 2600 domain, SuperMarioBros and deep sea. Overall, the proposed method could outperform Bootstrapped DQN, Double DQN and another recent bootstrapping baseline termed OB2I in majority of the testified domains.
reviews.main_review:
$\textbf{Strength}$: 
- This work tackles an important problem of exploiting randomized exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning problems.
- This paper is very well written and easy to follow.
- Overall the method of combining hypermodel with Q-network is quite sound and very general to be applied on deep RL problems.
- The empirical evaluation results are very extensive where relevant baselines are considered for comparison.

$\textbf{Weakness}$:
- Very limited novelty where the idea is from the work [1]. 
- During comparison, only results at intermediate training stage  (i.e., 20M frames)  is presented while the detailed scores for the full training is missing. So it is unclear if the method could really be the next state-of-the-art method for Atari 2600 test suite.
-Only limited number of baselines are considered. There is not decent exploration methods, such as UCB-type exploration or ramdomized exploration with noisy nets, being considered for comparison.  


$\textbf{Detailed comments}$:

1. My primary concern is about the novelty of this paper. I found that both the formulation for hypermodel and the loss function for optimizing the hypermodel is inherited from the existing work [1]. In [1], a more general formulation for the hypermodel has been discussed which includes linear models, neural models and additive prior models. In HyperDQN, a linear hypermodel is adopted where there is nothing new for the method. Thus the contribution of this paper is not in terms of introducing a new method but is to show additional experimental results for an existing method on several deep RL domains. I feel such contribution might not be significant enough to be published as a full paper in ICLR.

2. This paper tackles the policy learning problem from a direction of considering randomized exploration method. It claims the randomized exploration method O2BI as a SOTA exploration method but I do not think so. Generally, the O2BI as well as HyperDQN is only compared with limited baselines when the training is incomplete. I feel that many other variants of UCB-type algorithms, such as noisy net [2] might be able to outperform O2BI or HyperDQN greatly with 200M training budget. For instance, prediction-error based exploration methods could effectively progress on the extremely hard exploration task Montezuma's Revenge while O2BI and HyperDQN make no progress on it. Therefore, a more inclusive exploration methods for discussion/comparison is desired. 

3. The training curves presented in Fig 12 come with no error bars and/or O2BI is missing from the curves.

4. I feel noisy net is also a related randomized exploration model which should be considered for discussion and/or experimental comparison.

[1] HYPERMODELS FOR EXPLORATION
[2] NOISY NETWORKS FOR EXPLORATION

This paper is a well written one with extensive experimental results. 

The novelty of this method is rather limited as the method is exactly the same as the one proposed in a prior work. 

The empirical evaluation results appear to be less convincing to me because the results on Atari 2600 are only shown for intermediate model trained after 20M frames and the full training scores (inferred from the learning curves in Fig 12) appear to be much inferior than another variant of randomized exploration model which is the noisy net.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a method for randomized exploration in deep reinforcement learning by combining a linear hypermodel with a standard Q-network. The approach is evaluated across a suite of tasks—including Atari games, SuperMarioBros, and the Deep Sea environment—and is shown to yield improvements over select baselines at 20 million training frames. The writing is clear and the overall experimental setup is extensive.  

Decision  
3: Reject (not good enough)  

Strengths  
1. Tackles an important and timely challenge: efficient randomized exploration in deep RL.  
2. The manuscript is well written, with a clear presentation of the algorithm and its implementation.  
3. The proposed hybrid of hypermodel and Q-network is sound and general, easily applicable across multiple domains.  
4. A broad empirical evaluation is carried out, demonstrating performance on Atari benchmarks, SuperMarioBros, and the Deep Sea environment.  

Weaknesses  
1. Limited novelty. The core formulation and loss function are directly inherited from prior “Hypermodels for Exploration” work, with no substantial new theoretical or algorithmic contribution beyond applying an existing linear hypermodel to Q-learning.  
2. Incomplete training results. All comparisons are performed at an intermediate checkpoint of 20 M frames, yet many deep RL works report full-scale results at 200 M frames. Without the full learning curves, it is unclear whether the proposed method truly attains or surpasses state-of-the-art performance.  
3. Insufficient baseline comparisons. Key randomized exploration approaches are missing, notably UCB-type algorithms, NoisyNet, and prediction-error based exploration techniques. This omission makes it difficult to assess whether the gains hinge on the hypermodel or could be achieved by simpler or well-established methods.  
4. Figure 12 (training curves) is missing error bars, and the OB2I baseline is absent from these plots, reducing confidence in the stability and relative ranking of methods.  
5. Results on challenging sparse-reward tasks (e.g., Montezuma’s Revenge) remain unconvincing, in part because full-scale training curves and broader baselines are not provided.  

Main Points  
- The hypermodel’s loss and architectural choices are largely taken from previous work, so the manuscript does not clearly articulate its novel contribution to exploration.  
- By evaluating only at 20 M frames and omitting error bars, the paper leaves open whether improvements persist under longer training or whether they are significant.  
- The absence of strong exploration baselines such as UCB-type methods, NoisyNet, and prediction-error explorers weakens the empirical claims.  
- Without comprehensive results on notoriously hard tasks like Montezuma’s Revenge, the method’s effectiveness on truly sparse-reward domains is still undemonstrated.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Report full training curves up to 200 M frames (or comparable budgets) for all tasks to substantiate state-of-the-art claims.  
2. Incorporate additional strong exploration baselines, in particular UCB-based approaches, NoisyNet, and prediction-error based explorers, to provide a more thorough empirical comparison.  
3. Add error bars to all training or evaluation plots, and ensure that every baseline (e.g., OB2I) is included in the figures.  
4. Explicitly clarify what aspects of the hypermodel application or integration are new, and how they differ from the prior “Hypermodels for Exploration” formulation.  

While the paper is well executed in terms of clarity and breadth of evaluation, the limited novelty and gaps in empirical comparison prevent me from recommending acceptance in its current form.

title: Learning Synthetic Environments and Reward Networks for Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces Synthetic Environments and Synthetic Rewards as a bi-level optimization problem. The main idea is to find SEs and REs such that an RL agent trained under them will do well in a given, known target environment, without further training.
reviews.main_review:
Results:
- In Figure 2, the performance seems very unstable: In particular, the reward for individual agents seems to fluctuate widely and even the average starts drooping after the 50th iteration in the arcobot task. It would be great to know what the source of this instability is. 

Conceptual:
- There are repeated claims in the paper that the synthetic environment "focuses the learning" on the relevant states or learns "representations" of the target environment ("an informed representation of the target environment by
modifying the state distributions to bias agents towards relevant states"). This claim is entirely unclear to me at the moment. In particular, there simply doesn't seem to be any reason that the SE should have much in common with the ground truth environment. The only condition is that _optimal policies_ for the two environments match. In particular, there will be many environments in which the optimal policy is likely much easier to learn than in the original Env. 
For example, you can imagine a state transition dynamic that is entirely random but provides a large reward for the optimal action in each of the given states. Another option is to learn environment dynamics where the optimal action in each state deterministically transitions to an arbitrary next state, such that the agent will see all required states during training. 
It would be great to provide any type of evidence that there is actually semantic meaning in the SE that matches the underlying ground truth environment.  

Other comments:
- In Figure 2, it seems like the legend is switched? I.e. dashed line should be the solved threshold?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
A very interesting paper with early results in a potentially interesting direction. It is currently unclear what this method allows to do that was previously impossible from a practical point of view, but there seems to be promise. 
I'd be open to raising my score if my concerns are addressed by the authors. 



Update: 
Based on the discussion phase and the rebuttal I have updated the score to a 6. I believe this paper has enough merit to be published at ICLR, if there is space. It is an intriguing piece of work, even though the practical utility is very unclear at this point.

reviews.text:
Review for “Bi-Level Optimization of Synthetic Environments and Rewards for Efficient RL Training”

Summary  
This paper proposes a novel bi-level optimization framework that jointly learns synthetic environments and reward functions to accelerate reinforcement learning (RL). At the inner level, standard RL agents are trained in a learned synthetic MDP; at the outer level, the synthetic dynamics and rewards are updated to maximize performance on the target real environment. The authors present preliminary experiments on classic control tasks, showing that their learned synthetic setups can reduce the number of real-environment interactions needed to reach a performance threshold. Overall, the idea of synthesizing both dynamics and rewards via a bi-level formulation is interesting and opens a new direction for data-efficient RL. My final recommendation is a weak accept (6/10).

Strengths  
1. Novelty of Bi-Level Formulation: The paper introduces an intriguing approach that simultaneously learns synthetic transition models and reward functions through nested optimization, which to my knowledge has not been explored in prior work on efficient RL.  
2. Promising Preliminary Results: The experiments on tasks like Acrobot demonstrate that the method can indeed lower the interaction cost in the true environment, at least in early to mid training phases. This provides encouraging evidence that the framework could lead to more sample-efficient RL.

Weaknesses  
1. Experimental Stability: In Figure 2 (Acrobot), the individual-agent reward curves show very high variance, and notably the average reward starts to decline after about the 50th outer iteration. Such wide fluctuations and the subsequent performance drop call into question the stability and reliability of the proposed algorithm.  
2. Conceptual Justification: The core claim is that the learned synthetic environments capture “informed representations” of the real task’s relevant states and dynamics. However, matching the optimal policy on the real task does not guarantee any semantic or structural similarity between synthetic and real MDPs. In principle, trivial or degenerate dynamics could suffice to produce identical optimal actions without bearing meaningful correspondence to the true environment.  
3. Practical Utility Over Prior Art: It remains unclear what concrete advantages this bi-level synthesis of both models and rewards provides relative to established model-based methods or existing reward-shaping and curriculum learning techniques. The paper does not convincingly demonstrate what new applications or scaling benefits the approach enables beyond modest gains on benchmark control problems.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Analyze Instability Sources: Perform an ablation or diagnostic analysis to isolate why policy performance in Figure 2 oscillates so heavily and why the average reward deteriorates after iteration 50. Possible factors include overly aggressive outer updates or misaligned objectives—clarifying this would greatly strengthen confidence in the method.  
2. Demonstrate Semantic Correspondence: Include experiments or visualizations showing that the learned synthetic dynamics and rewards align with interpretable aspects of the real environment (e.g., state embeddings, reward contours). This could involve comparing feature activations or transition structures to verify that the synthetic MDP is not merely a policy shortcut.  
3. Clarify Figure 2 Legend: The line purportedly marking the “solved threshold” in Figure 2 appears mislabeled or misplaced. Please correct the legend to accurately indicate the target performance level for the Acrobot task.  

Recommendation  
Weak Accept (6/10).  
This work proposes an original optimization framework for synthesizing environments and rewards that has the potential to advance sample-efficient RL. However, further clarifications regarding experimental stability, conceptual grounding, and comparative utility are necessary to fully establish its impact. I recommend acceptance pending revision to address the above weaknesses.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.
reviews.main_review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.

Main Review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

Summary Of The Review:
This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel meta-learning framework that synthesizes proxy models for both environment dynamics and reward functions, using an outer loop powered by Evolution Strategies (ES) and an inner loop of standard RL algorithms (e.g., DDQN, Dueling DDQN, discrete TD3). By training RL agents on these learned synthetic environments and reward networks, the authors demonstrate consistent improvements in downstream task performance on a suite of control benchmarks. The work is notable for its methodological rigor, extensive hyperparameter tuning, and commitments to reproducibility through public code and detailed appendices.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Thoroughness: The manuscript is exceptionally well written, walking the reader through design motivations, optimization choices, and hyperparameter settings with great care. Potential questions are anticipated and answered directly in the text.  
2. Methodological Rigor: Every architectural and algorithmic choice is justified, from the use of ES in the outer loop to the specific RL algorithms in the inner loop. The appendices provide exhaustive detail on experimental setups.  
3. Reproducibility: Code release and extensive supplementary material ensure that others can replicate and build on these results with minimal friction.  
4. Innovative yet Simple Core Idea: By borrowing logic from prior meta-learning and model-based RL work, the paper distills a clear central contribution: synthetic environments and learned reward networks can be co-optimized to accelerate agent training.  
5. Honest Limitation Discussion: The authors are transparent about their reliance on ES for meta-optimization and acknowledge its computational cost, fostering trust in their results.

Weaknesses  
1. Density of Detail: While the thoroughness is commendable, the paper can feel overwhelming in places due to the volume of methodological and experimental detail. This density occasionally impedes the flow for readers less familiar with every component.  
2. Hand-Wavy Hypotheses: Some of the mechanistic explanations—such as the notion of “efficient synthetic state dynamics”—are presented without sufficient empirical or theoretical grounding. These hypotheses would benefit from deeper analysis.  
3. Limited Task Diversity: Experiments focus on relatively simple control tasks, leaving open the question of how well the approach scales to more complex or high-dimensional environments.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Consider restructuring the manuscript into two complementary parts—one focusing on the ES-based meta-optimization framework and another on the reward network synthesis—to improve readability and modularity.  
2. Strengthen support for the key claim that meta-learning synthetic environments accelerates RL training. This could take the form of additional ablations, theoretical insights, or linking observed phenomena to established RL theory.  
3. Fix the inverted labels in Figure 2, where the dotted lines should correctly mark the thresholds described in the caption.  
4. Augment Section 6’s claim that learning reward functions is generally easier than learning transition models with citations or counter-examples, as this statement may not hold universally across MDPs.

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper sets a high bar for clarity, thoroughness, and reproducibility in meta-learning research. Its simple yet powerful idea of co-optimizing synthetic environments and reward networks offers a promising direction for accelerating RL agent training. Despite some narrative density and under-supported hypotheses, the work’s strengths far outweigh its weaknesses. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 8 – good paper).

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes to learn neural environment models they term Synthetic Environments, such that planning in the model with model-free RL results in good performance in the real environment.
The authors study learning full models as well as reward-only models they call Reward Networks, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.
reviews.main_review:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation. The exposition of the method and description of experiments are quite clear and complete. However, there are a few weaknesses.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SE’s could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments.
Most applications are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment.
However, the problem formulation in eqn (1) describes maximising performance absolutely.
This is approximated in Alg. 1 with a generic TrainAgent function that approximates the argmax_theta. But it seems the specific TrainAgent functions chosen are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an argmax.
The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalisation of SE’s to different algorithms and their hyperparameters, and even address generalisation algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions, rather than being central to the method.
It feels that to use SE’s for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms.
Further, simply maximising the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms.
It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms.
However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment?

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimisation). NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. They argue that models learned only to further optimisation of a policy (as in this work's problem statement) may perform better than traditional models when capacity is limited. However, the ES-based bi-level optimisation is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper proposes learning Synthetic Environments (SEs) alongside Reward Networks (RNs) as proxy models for reinforcement learning. The approach formulates a bi-level optimization (Eqn 1) in which an outer loop—solved via Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) perturbations—seeks SE parameters and additive potential‐based reward shapers that maximize real‐environment cumulative reward. The inner loop employs a TrainAgent subroutine to approximate the optimal policy under the proxy. Experiments on simple benchmark tasks demonstrate feasibility and a degree of robustness to different inner‐loop algorithms and hyperparameters.

Strengths  
1. Novelty: Introducing SEs coupled with learned RNs as proxy environments and reward analyzers is an original idea in model-based RL.  
2. Clarity: The method, bi-level optimization, and experimental protocol are described in a clear, step-by-step fashion.  
3. Robustness Analysis: The authors test their framework across multiple inner-loop learners and hyperparameter settings, underscoring algorithmic stability.  
4. Inductive Bias Exploration: The paper explores potential-based reward shaping, giving insight into how additive RNs influence learning dynamics.

Weaknesses  
1. Misaligned Motivation and Formulation: While the paper emphasizes high-level applications such as AutoML, robotics tuning, and agent analysis—where preserving relative algorithmic rankings is crucial—the core objective in Eqn 1 strictly maximizes absolute performance. This creates a gap between the stated goals (ranking correlation) and the mathematical formulation (reward maximization).  
2. Inner-Loop Generality: The TrainAgent implementation approximates an arg max over policy parameters in a way that is not algorithm-agnostic, meaning the choice of optimizer heavily shapes the learned SE and RN.  
3. Limited Empirical Scope: All experiments are conducted on very simple control benchmarks. It remains unclear whether the method generalizes to more complex domains or unseen algorithms and hyperparameter settings—the very scenarios the paper claims to address.  
4. Arbitrary RN Objective: The current RN loss includes a clipping term at a fixed solution threshold (w_sol·max(0, C_sol–C_fin)), which feels ad hoc and may not promote optimal reward shaping across the training trajectory.  
5. Computational Cost vs. Benefit: ES-based bi-level optimization is computationally expensive, and the paper does not convincingly demonstrate its advantage over standard supervised dynamics or reward modeling approaches.

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Reformulate the Bi-Level Objective: Extend Eqn 1 to explicitly incorporate multiple inner-loop algorithms or hyperparameter settings, targeting not only absolute reward but also the preservation of relative performance rankings.  
2. Define a Ranking-Based Fitness: Introduce a fitness function that measures correlation (e.g., Spearman’s ρ) between algorithm/hyperparameter rankings in the SE versus the real environment.  
3. Empirical Correlation Analysis: Conduct experiments that directly measure whether algorithm or hyperparameter performance in the learned SE predicts real‐environment outcomes, thus validating the ranking objective.  
4. Reward Shaping Metric: Replace or augment the current threshold‐clipping RN loss with an area‐under‐curve criterion over the entire inner‐loop learning reward trajectory to encourage smooth, consistent shaping.  
5. Fixed Hyperparameter Baselines: Evaluate SE performance under fixed inner-loop settings to quantify the generalization gap—report results for a held-out set of hyperparameters or even unseen optimizers.  
6. Figure and Table Clarity: Improve visualizations by smoothing or subsampling noisy learning curves, grouping comparable bar charts, and clearly annotating the experiments that test hyperparameter or algorithm generalization.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper introduces an intriguing direction by jointly learning proxy environments and reward networks, and it is presented with commendable clarity. However, the current formulation and empirical evaluation leave open questions about alignment between goals and objectives, scalability to complex tasks, and the efficacy of the ES-based approach relative to simpler baselines. I find the contribution to be marginally above the acceptance threshold, contingent on the authors addressing the alignment of their problem statement with the actual optimization goal and strengthening their empirical validation along the lines suggested above.  

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.

Summary Of The Review:
While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper’s idea of learning proxy environments and reward networks for RL interesting and clearly presented, but raises concerns about the alignment of the stated motivation and applications with the problem formulation and empirical evaluation, as well as the choice of objectives and the simplicity of tested environments.
strengths: Novel concept of Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs) for proxy model–based RL
Clear and complete exposition of the method and experimental setup
Investigation of robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters
Exploration of inductive biases in reward shaping (e.g., potential-based formulations)
weaknesses: Motivation and proposed applications (AutoML, robotics, agent analysis) not well aligned with problem formulation or experiments
Problem formulation (Eqn 1) focuses on absolute performance maximization rather than correlating relative algorithm performance
TrainAgent implementation is not a generic argmax and strongly influences outcomes
Experiments limited to very simple benchmark tasks, making generalization unclear
RN training objective (clipping at solution threshold) is arbitrary and may produce suboptimal shaping
ES-based bi-level optimization is computationally expensive and its advantage over supervised model learning is unclear
suggestions: Reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for multiple inner-loop algorithms and their relative performance
Consider a fitness function that captures correlations in algorithm/hyperparameter rankings between SE and real environment
Empirically measure whether performance of different HPs/algorithms in SEs correlates with their performance in the real environment
Evaluate RNs using area-under-curve returns instead of clipping at an arbitrary reward threshold
Report performance of fixed hyperparameters on SEs under the same fixed settings to quantify generalization gap
Improve figure clarity by smoothing/subsampling curves and grouping comparable bars together
main_points: The stated high-level applications depend on preserving relative algorithmic rankings in the SE, but the current objective maximizes absolute reward
Generalization to unseen algorithms and hyperparameters is only briefly addressed rather than integrated into the core formulation
Reward Networks formulated as additive potential shapers are intuitively appealing but did not consistently outperform baselines
Evolution strategy–based bi-level optimization is powerful but expensive relative to supervised dynamics modeling
Empirical tests are a feasibility study on small benchmarks and leave open whether the method scales
technical_details: Problem formulation (Eqn 1) is a bi-level optimization maximizing real-environment cumulative reward
TrainAgent approximates the inner-loop argmax θ* and is not algorithm-agnostic in practice
Reward Network objective includes velocity of convergence term: n_tr + w_sol·max(0,C_sol–C_fin)
Suggested alternative objective: optimize area under the training reward curve for the full inner loop
Evolution Strategies (NES) used in outer loop with population perturbations ψ_i = ψ + ε_i

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper aims to learn proxy environments (synthetic environments or SEs) and reward functions (reward networks or RNs), parameterized as neural networks, such that these proxy models provide beneficial transitions to make it more sample-efficient to learn a policy for a fixed target environment (referred to as the real environment). SEs replace the observed state and rewards during training, while reward networks provide a synthetic reward that is a function of the real reward, current state, and next state. The proposed method formulates this problem as a bi-level optimization, where the inner loop consists of standard RL under the proxy model, and the outer loop consists of NES with the aim of optimizing either the performance on the true, target environment (SEs) or a the number of training steps needed to reach a certain return threshold on the real environment.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations).
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward. These parameterizations are not studied in combination with SEs.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network."
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training.
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. In contrast the SE objective optimizes for performance. This seems like a significant inconsistency between the training methology for the two types of models, making it hard to compare the relative merits of SEs and RNs.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. This would directly separate out the performance gains due to training on the  SE.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics, the case made for these methods is weak, and the experimental results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. Given the additional compute required by these methods, it seems that various forms of policy distillation or even training from scratch seem more efficient. Further, it is not clear why only certain formulations (SEs and RNs) of meta-learning neural transition dynamics were investigated, while others (like a state-only neural transition network) were not. Given these points, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to learn proxy environments (synthetic environments or SEs) and reward functions (reward networks or RNs), parameterized as neural networks, such that these proxy models provide beneficial transitions to make it more sample-efficient to learn a policy for a fixed target environment (referred to as the real environment). SEs replace the observed state and rewards during training, while reward networks provide a synthetic reward that is a function of the real reward, current state, and next state. The proposed method formulates this problem as a bi-level optimization, where the inner loop consists of standard RL under the proxy model, and the outer loop consists of NES with the aim of optimizing either the performance on the true, target environment (SEs) or a the number of training steps needed to reach a certain return threshold on the real environment.

Main Review:
### Strengths
- This paper extends prior work on meta-learning MDP transition dynamics for improving training performance on a target environment, by introducing a method that aims to learn both the state and reward transitions. This formulation is more general than prior works.
- Experimental results are provided for multiple formulations of meta-learned MDP transition dynamics, spanning both joint state and reward networks (SEs) as well as various formulations of a reward network (potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations).
- This paper includes experiments on transferring to different agent architectures and hyperparameter settings, which seems to be a novel experimental setting for meta-learning MDP dynamics.

### Weaknesses
- The motivation for learning SEs and RNs for singleton target environments is not convincing. While there seem to be some marginal gains in sample-efficiency, it would seem these gains are largely made irrelevant due to the additional training needed to train the environment in the first place. Moreover, if SEs and RNs require first training the agent multiple times on synthetic environments, why not just copy weights or use a form of policy distillation, e.g. kickstarting (Schmitt et al, 2018), rather than train the agent again? Further, it seems the basic ICM baseline already matches or outperforms the proposed RN approaches across experimental settings, further weakening the argument for using the more computationally expensive method proposed in this work.
- Given the wide design space of possible ways of learning the state and reward transition dynamics for a target real environment, it is unclear why only these two (SE and RN) formulations were studied. Given that this is an empirical paper, the value of the findings rests largely on a clean experimental design that provides a convincing recommendation based on comprehensive experiments. The seemingly arbitrary choice for only considering the SE and RN formulation does not provide such a convincing recommendation for using either of these formulations.
- RNs are effectively an ablation of SEs, where the state is not learned. However, the current experimental design does not neatly present these results as a clean ablation. This is because the RN experiments also separately consider various kinds of potential and non-potential-based reward shaping parameterizations for the learned reward. These parameterizations are not studied in combination with SEs.
- Likewise, there is no corresponding ablation for when the reward is not learned, but only the state is learned, i.e. a "state-transition network" or perhaps a "state-augmentation network."
- It is not made clear why exactly SEs and RNs have to use different objectives when training.
- The RN objective seems strange, as it seems to be optimizing for sample efficiency rather than performance. In contrast the SE objective optimizes for performance. This seems like a significant inconsistency between the training methology for the two types of models, making it hard to compare the relative merits of SEs and RNs.
- The reward difference term of the RN objective also caps the maximum performance improvement compared to training on the real environment. The motivation for this objective design is not clear, as not taking the max between the reward difference and 0 should encourage learning RNs that lead to outperforming training on the real environment, rather than simply matching it.
- Further, it seems that there is not a consistent ranking in terms of which parameterization for the RNs improves training performance on the real environment the most, and similarly, no consistent ranking between non-transfer and transfer scenarios for each environment.
- Meanwhile, the SE results on CartPole and Acrobot would benefit from a direct comparison to the training curves for agents using the best hyperparameters, trained on the real environment. This would directly separate out the performance gains due to training on the  SE.
- The comparison to previous work such as Zheng et al, 2020 seems quite hand-wavey. Zheng et al, 2020 in particular is quite similar to this work in terms of analyzing the effect of learning an RN and also includes more comprehensive experimental results in a more complex setting requiring recurrent RNs. The novel contributions in relation to these prior works seems to be around analyzing the impact of varying agent hyperparameters on transferring to alternative agent settings and architectures during evaluation. However, these transfer results do not seem consistently strong for any one method proposed.
- Sampling agent hyperparameter settings from a diverse range seems crucial for the learned SEs and RNs to transfer to other agent configurations. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the proposed methods are to the agent hyperparameter ranges used during training.

Summary Of The Review:
While this work proposes an interesting generalization of prior work on meta-learning MDP dynamics, the case made for these methods is weak, and the experimental results—especially for SEs—are uncompelling. Given the additional compute required by these methods, it seems that various forms of policy distillation or even training from scratch seem more efficient. Further, it is not clear why only certain formulations (SEs and RNs) of meta-learning neural transition dynamics were investigated, while others (like a state-only neural transition network) were not. Given these points, I recommend this paper in its current form for rejection.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

Response:

title: FP-DETR: Detection Transformer Advanced by Fully Pre-training

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes a novel transformer-based detection algorithm (FP-DETR) that could benefit from large-scale pre-training for model robustness and generalization on small datasets. FP-DETR consists of a encoder only transformer on top of a set of lightweight feature extracting layers for pre-training. It is then fine-tuned with a task-adapter that helps the model attending to object location as well as be aware of inter-object relationship for detection task. Evaluation is done on both large scale dataset COCO and relatively small domain CityScapes with ablation analysis on different system modules and design choices. The result indicates that FP-DETR presents better generalization capabilities on smaller dataset with on-part performance compared to other SOTA in general settings.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, I like the paper but I feel hesitant to accept this paper at its current status . While it is no secret that one can improve model robustness and generalizability through pre-training, I do believe that the method contributes values to the community with an architecture that is designed to better utilize imageNet classification pre-training and close the gap between classification and detection in the fine-tuning stage. However, I found the current experimental protocol is not solid enough to support some of the claimed contributions. I am happy to change my rating if the author could provide justification through discussion or additional experiments. 

Pros:

- The paper is well written and easy to read. The intuition behind the algorithm design is sufficiently discussed. The technical session is described in detail.
- I like the idea of decreasing the model discrepancy between upstream classification tasks and downstream detection tasks through an encoder only transformer. 
- The intuition to provide location attention and inter-object relationship attention is interesting
- The overall validity of the algorithm is backed by performance close to SOTA on COCO and outstanding in Cityscapes. 

Cons:
- The main concern I have in the experiment is whether we can decouple the advantage of the proposed architecture or the fact that the Transformer encoder is being pretrained when compared to other SOTA (Table 2). E.g. What’s the performance of the proposed algorithm with normal ResNet backbones? What’s the performance of Deformable Tranformer with Encoder pretrained？
- The ablation analysis on the lite version (Table 3) is not as strong as one in the base version. It is easier to improve on top of a weak performance.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison decreases the strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a novel transformer-based detection algorithm (FP-DETR) that could benefit from large-scale pre-training for model robustness and generalization on small datasets. FP-DETR consists of a encoder only transformer on top of a set of lightweight feature extracting layers for pre-training. It is then fine-tuned with a task-adapter that helps the model attending to object location as well as be aware of inter-object relationship for detection task. Evaluation is done on both large scale dataset COCO and relatively small domain CityScapes with ablation analysis on different system modules and design choices. The result indicates that FP-DETR presents better generalization capabilities on smaller dataset with on-part performance compared to other SOTA in general settings.

Main Review:
Overall, I like the paper but I feel hesitant to accept this paper at its current status . While it is no secret that one can improve model robustness and generalizability through pre-training, I do believe that the method contributes values to the community with an architecture that is designed to better utilize imageNet classification pre-training and close the gap between classification and detection in the fine-tuning stage. However, I found the current experimental protocol is not solid enough to support some of the claimed contributions. I am happy to change my rating if the author could provide justification through discussion or additional experiments. 

Pros:

- The paper is well written and easy to read. The intuition behind the algorithm design is sufficiently discussed. The technical session is described in detail.
- I like the idea of decreasing the model discrepancy between upstream classification tasks and downstream detection tasks through an encoder only transformer. 
- The intuition to provide location attention and inter-object relationship attention is interesting
- The overall validity of the algorithm is backed by performance close to SOTA on COCO and outstanding in Cityscapes. 

Cons:
- The main concern I have in the experiment is whether we can decouple the advantage of the proposed architecture or the fact that the Transformer encoder is being pretrained when compared to other SOTA (Table 2). E.g. What’s the performance of the proposed algorithm with normal ResNet backbones? What’s the performance of Deformable Tranformer with Encoder pretrained？
- The ablation analysis on the lite version (Table 3) is not as strong as one in the base version. It is easier to improve on top of a weak performance.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison decreases the strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “FP-DETR: Bridging Classification and Detection via an Encoder-Only Transformer and Task Adapter”

Summary  
This paper presents FP-DETR, an object detector that reuses an encoder-only transformer pre-trained on ImageNet and a lightweight task adapter to narrow the gap between classification and detection. The authors argue that by prompting the encoder with location priors and modeling inter-object relationships in the adapter, one can better transfer pre-trained features to the detection task. Experiments on COCO show competitive AP, and additional results on Cityscapes indicate strong generalization.

Strengths  
1. The manuscript is very well written and structured, making the technical contributions easy to follow.  
2. The idea of leveraging an encoder-only transformer to exploit classification pre-training is novel in the context of DETR-like detectors.  
3. The location prompting strategy and task adapter for inter-object relationship modeling are intuitively motivated and clearly described.  
4. Empirical results demonstrate that FP-DETR achieves performance on par with recent benchmarks on COCO and generalizes effectively to Cityscapes.  
5. The detailed presentation of the model architecture and training protocol aids reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. It is unclear whether the performance gains arise from the architectural changes or simply from the extensive pre-training on ImageNet.  
2. The paper does not report results for FP-DETR using a standard convolutional backbone (e.g., ResNet), which would clarify the benefit of the encoder-only design.  
3. There is no direct comparison in which the encoder of a Deformable Transformer is pre-trained under the same protocol, making it difficult to assess the unique contribution of FP-DETR’s architecture.  
4. All ablation studies are performed on the “lite” variant, which is a relatively weak baseline; ablations on the stronger base model are missing.  
5. The claim that pre-training improves robustness and generalization, while valid, is not particularly novel.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• Backbone Comparison: Please include results for FP-DETR when paired with a conventional ResNet backbone. This will help determine how much the encoder-only transformer itself contributes to the final performance versus the detection head.  
• Pre-trained Deformable Transformer Baseline: Evaluate a Deformable Transformer detector whose encoder has been pre-trained under the same ImageNet scheme. A head-to-head comparison would isolate the effect of your task adapter and prompting mechanism.  
• Expanded Ablations: Conduct ablation studies on the base (stronger) version of FP-DETR, not only the lite model. This will strengthen confidence in the architectural choices.  
• Decoupling Architecture and Pre-training: Consider experiments that freeze the pre-trained encoder while training the adapter alone, or vice versa, to more clearly attribute gains to each component.  
• Clarify Novelty of Pre-training Claim: It would help to position your robustness/generalization arguments with respect to existing work that also leverages pre-training for detection tasks.

Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
FP-DETR offers an interesting architecture and shows promising results, but further experiments are needed to fully validate that the improvements stem from the proposed design rather than from the pre-training strategy alone.

The paper proposed a novel architecture to empower a transformer-based object detector with smoother pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm. The paper is well-written and provides interesting discussion on its intuition and new perspective on positional embeddings in this context. However, pre-training helps improve the model is not a novel theory by itself. The insufficient experimental comparison decreases the strength of the paper. I am looking forward to seeing the rebuttal and willing to change my rating accordingly.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

The proposed framework achieves competitive performance and becomes more robust and generalizable.

Main Review:
- This work introduced the ‘prompting’ to detection task. By mitigating the gap between pre-training and finetuning task, it shows a better performance on COCO dataset. reviews.text:
Review for “Visual Prompting for Detection Transformers”

Summary  
This paper introduces the notion of visual prompting—specifically, the use of learnable query positional embeddings—to better align pre-training and fine-tuning in DETR-style object detectors. By extending the single-class-token design to 300 prompt tokens (FP-EncDec), the authors show consistent gains on COCO detection, enhanced robustness under common corruptions, and improved few-shot generalization. A suite of ablation studies further supports key design decisions.

Strengths  
- Novel adaptation of prompting from NLP to object detection, leveraging multiple learnable query embeddings to bridge upstream and downstream tasks.  
- Demonstrated performance improvements on COCO: the multi-token FP-EncDec architecture outperforms both the single-token variant and standard baselines.  
- Empirical evidence of increased robustness to image corruptions and strong generalization to small-scale/few-shot settings.  
- Thorough ablation studies (e.g., Table 7) validating the impact of using 300 query tokens versus a single token and illustrating the task adapter’s ability to capture inter-object relationships.

Weaknesses  
- The paper does not explore pre-training FP-DETR itself with 300 prompt tokens; all 300-token experiments appear restricted to fine-tuning only.  
- An ablation study using a standard ResNet-50 backbone in Table 2 is missing, leaving open questions about the approach’s backbone-agnostic benefits.  
- A minor typographical error in the Introduction refers to “PT-DETR” rather than “FP-DETR.”

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Pre-train FP-DETR with 300 query tokens and report both upstream (pre-training) and downstream (fine-tuning) metrics to confirm that the prompting mechanism yields benefits throughout training.  
2. Include an additional ablation in Table 2 where the FP-DETR variants are evaluated with a ResNet-50 backbone, to demonstrate that gains persist beyond transformer-only backbones.  
3. Correct the typo “PT-DETR” to “FP-DETR” in the Introduction and perform a pass over the manuscript to catch any other minor errors.

Minor Comments  
- Line XX: “PT-DETR” → “FP-DETR.”  
- Please ensure consistent notation when referring to the task adapter and prompt tokens.

Overall Assessment  
This work presents a timely and creative extension of prompting techniques to object detection transformers, yielding measurable improvements across detection accuracy, robustness, and low-data regimes. While a few additional experiments would further solidify the contribution, the current results are competitive and the core idea is sound. I recommend marginal acceptance (rating 6) pending the suggested ablations and typo fixes.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper argues that recently developed detection transformers didn't employ pre-training on transformer layers and not benefit from pretraining. Hence, it proposes FP-DETR to fully pretrain transformer on image classification and then finetune it for object detection by changing the task adapter. Compared to previous methods, it only uses encoder-only transformers. Further experiments show reasonable performances and robustness analysis.
reviews.main_review:
This paper is well-organized and easy to follow. 

Strength: 
+ Encoder-only transformer design without the need of extra visual backbone
+ Lower parameters compared to concurrent works

Weakness:
- The effeteness of pre-training is not well justified. The paper claims "large-scale pre-training on ImageNet helps FP-DETR learn more generalizable representation." However, the author only conducts experiments on imagenet-1k, which barely counts a large-scale pre-training. In order to further prove the benefits, it would be best to re-run experiments on imagenet-22k and show the continuous improvement .
- The necessary of deformable convolution layer.  The encoder-only transformer design would be more appreciated if not using deformable convolution. How big will the impact be if trained without deformable convolution? 
- Latency. Since author focuses on small models, it would be best to report latency.  
- The robustness analysis is not convincing and not related to the main contribution. The author should include more ablation studies on hyper-parameters instead (such as #encoder, #dim, #token, etc)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper shares some insights on the proposed FP-DETR, but it lacks comprehensive experiments to justify the claims. After reading the rebuttal and extra ablation studies, most of my questions have been answered. I believe this paper has some merit and worth accepting for a poster. Hence, I have raised my score.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper argues that recently developed detection transformers didn't employ pre-training on transformer layers and not benefit from pretraining. Hence, it proposes FP-DETR to fully pretrain transformer on image classification and then finetune it for object detection by changing the task adapter. Compared to previous methods, it only uses encoder-only transformers. Further experiments show reasonable performances and robustness analysis.

Main Review:
This paper is well-organized and easy to follow. 

Strength: 
+ Encoder-only transformer design without the need of extra visual backbone
+ Lower parameters compared to concurrent works

Weakness:
- The effeteness of pre-training is not well justified. The paper claims "large-scale pre-training on ImageNet helps FP-DETR learn more generalizable representation." However, the author only conducts experiments on imagenet-1k, which barely counts a large-scale pre-training. In order to further prove the benefits, it would be best to re-run experiments on imagenet-22k and show the continuous improvement .
- The necessary of deformable convolution layer.  The encoder-only transformer design would be more appreciated if not using deformable convolution. How big will the impact be if trained without deformable convolution? 
- Latency. Since author focuses on small models, it would be best to report latency.  
- The robustness analysis is not convincing and not related to the main contribution. The author should include more ablation studies on hyper-parameters instead (such as #encoder, #dim, #token, etc)

Summary Of The Review:
This paper shares some insights on the proposed FP-DETR, but it lacks comprehensive experiments to justify the claims. After reading the rebuttal and extra ablation studies, most of my questions have been answered. I believe this paper has some merit and worth accepting for a poster. Hence, I have raised my score.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents an encoder-only transformer architecture for vision tasks that entirely removes the need for a separate convolutional backbone. By integrating a deformable convolution layer for patch embedding and relying solely on transformer encoder blocks, the model achieves competitive performance with fewer parameters than many concurrent approaches. The manuscript is well organized, clearly written, and the authors have addressed many of the initial concerns in their rebuttal, making the work poster-worthy.

Strengths  
• Encoder-only design: The proposal does away with an external visual backbone, simplifying the overall pipeline.  
• Parameter efficiency: Across comparable settings, the method uses significantly fewer parameters than recent detector and segmentation transformers.

Weaknesses  
1. Pre-training validation is limited. The claimed benefits of large-scale pre-training rest solely on experiments with ImageNet-1k. It remains unclear whether performance will continue to improve when scaled up.  
2. Deformable convolution justification. The necessity and contribution of the deformable convolution layer are not separately quantified, leaving its impact ambiguous.  
3. Missing latency measurements. For lightweight variants especially, inference speed is a key practical metric but is not reported.  
4. Robustness analysis. The current robustness experiments do not tie back to the core architectural contributions and feel tangential rather than integral.  
5. Incomplete hyper-parameter ablations. Critical design choices—number of encoder layers, embedding dimensionality, number of query tokens—have not been systematically examined.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Extend pre-training to a larger dataset such as ImageNet-22k to confirm that scaling continues to yield improvements.  
• Include an ablation without the deformable convolution module to isolate its effect on accuracy and speed.  
• Measure and report inference latency for all model sizes, with particular focus on the smaller variants.  
• Refocus robustness experiments or replace them with analyses directly probing how the encoder-only design handles common perturbations.  
• Perform comprehensive ablations over key hyper-parameters (encoder depth, embedding dimension, query count) to better guide practitioners.

Conclusion  
Overall, this work introduces an elegant, parameter-efficient transformer architecture for vision that is well explained and shows promise. While I have reservations about the depth of experimental validation—particularly regarding large-scale pre-training, the role of deformable convolutions, latency profiling, and hyper-parameter studies—I believe these issues can be addressed without fundamentally altering the contribution. On balance, I recommend acceptance as a marginally above-threshold paper (Rating: 6), contingent on the authors strengthening their empirical evaluation along the lines suggested.
