reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.
reviews.main_review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes ZeroFL, a variant of the SWAT sparsification method adapted to the federated learning setting. By masking and transmitting only a subset of model weights, ZeroFL aims to reduce on-device training overhead and upload communication costs. The authors demonstrate a proof-of-concept on CIFAR-10 with 100 clients and report up to a 7.4× reduction in upload volume alongside comparable test accuracy.

Strengths  
1. The manuscript is clearly written and easy to follow; the overall flow and notation are well organized.  
2. The proposed modification is straightforward and simple to implement on top of existing SWAT code.  
3. The experiments provide an initial proof-of-concept that sparse weight updates can be effective in a federated CIFAR-10 setting.  
4. The method explicitly targets two important objectives in on-device FL: sparse local training and reduced upload communication.

Weaknesses  
1. Incremental Contribution: ZeroFL is essentially a direct extension of SWAT to FL with only minor protocol changes. The novelty over prior SWAT work is limited.  
2. Missing Baselines: Important federated sparsification methods—most notably federated dropout—are not included in the experimental comparison.  
3. Activation Sparsification: Although SWAT supports activation sparsification, this feature is mentioned but never leveraged in the reported experiments.  
4. Narrow Experimental Scope: All results are on CIFAR-10 with only 100 clients and a single random seed. There is no evidence of stability across multiple seeds or generality to other FL datasets (e.g., EMNIST/FEMNIST) or larger client populations.  
5. Communication Analysis: The main text does not discuss the extra overhead of sending sparse weight indices (e.g., CSR/CSC encoding). Moreover, the derivation of the “0.5×(r_mask – sp)” communication savings formula is omitted, and it is unclear how the 7.4× improvement in Table 1 is computed or which baseline it refers to.  
6. Privacy Claims: The paper asserts that client‐specific sparsity patterns provide privacy benefits, but this claim is under-motivated and may be misleading without a formal analysis.  
7. Upload-only Focus: While the upload channel is compressed, no strategies for download-side compression are explored.  
8. Presentation Clarity:  
   a. Algorithm 1 pseudocode is confusing, as it does not clearly distinguish among the three update variants (sparse, dense, activation‐sparse).  
   b. Figure 3 uses a poor color palette and low line contrast, making it hard to interpret.  
   c. Terminology is inconsistent (e.g., “top-K” vs. “top-K%” appear interchangeably).  
   d. Table 1’s caption cites a 7.4× improvement without specifying whether this is relative to dense FL, SWAT, or vanilla FedAvg.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Empirical Comparisons: Include federated dropout and other relevant baselines to contextualize ZeroFL’s performance.  
2. Leverage SWAT Fully: Clarify and, if feasible, integrate activation sparsification into the federated experiments.  
3. Broaden Experiments: Evaluate on additional datasets such as EMNIST/FEMNIST, scale up to more clients, and report results over multiple random seeds with variance or confidence intervals.  
4. Communication Overhead: In the main text, detail the cost of transmitting index data (e.g., CSR/CSC formats) and provide a transparent derivation of the communication savings formula.  
5. Privacy Discussion: Either strengthen the privacy argument with formal analysis or temper the claim to avoid potential misunderstanding.  
6. Download Compression: Investigate and report methods for compressing server-to-client model downloads.  
7. Pseudocode Refinement: Rewrite Algorithm 1 to include clear conditional branches for each update variant.  
8. Figure and Terminology Fixes: Adopt a more distinguishable color scheme and consistent line styles for plots; standardize on “top-K” or “top-K%” throughout.  
9. Table Caption Clarity: Explicitly state which baseline is used when claiming speedup or compression factors.

Overall Assessment  
ZeroFL presents a clean adaptation of sparse training techniques to the federated context and addresses a relevant problem in reducing communication and compute on edge devices. However, the paper’s incremental novelty, narrow empirical scope, missing baselines, and gaps in clarity undermine its current contribution. I recommend strengthening the experimental evaluation and presentation before reconsideration.

Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes ZeroFL; a method that allows for sparse neural network training at the edge along with reduced upload communication, both of which being important aspects in federated learning.  ZeroFL essentially follows a prior work on sparse neural network training, namely SWAT, and adapts it appropriately for the federated setting. The SWAT method works by setting a target weight sparsity percentage (denoted by $sp$) which is enforced in both the forward and backward pass by a top-k operation on the weights. This allows for using sparse convolutions and thus offering training time speed-ups. The authors note that it is important to use the same set of sparse weights for inference as well, in order to not affect performance. 

ZeroFL then applies this idea in the federated setting by letting each client perform SWAT type of training with a target sparsity of $sp$. Instead of the clients then communicating to the server the exact set of sparse weights obtained at the end of their local training procedure, ZeroFL proposes to add an extra “slack” $r_{mask}$ and communicate $1 - sp + r_{mask}$ percentage of weights (instead of $1 - sp$). The authors motivate this change via the results of an ablation study which showed significantly lower performance when directly applying SWAT to the federated setting. They argue that with the extra $r_{mask}$ weights, a “cleaner” training signal from the clients can be given as these weights are not “corrupted” by the weights of the other clients when averaging at the server. The authors further propose three separate weights of updating the server model, i.e., 1) using the client top-k weights, 2) using the difference between the server and client top-k weights and 3) using the top-k differences between the server and client weights.  

The authors experimentally validate the ZeroFL method on cifar 10 using 100 clients and both iid and non-iid splits.

Main Review:
This submission is reasonably well-written and easy to follow. The idea is simple to implement and seems to work, at least in this specific setting that the authors considered. This constitutes a nice advantage to ZeroFL. Nevertheless, I believe this submission needs quite a bit of work before I can recommend for acceptance:

- There are comparisons with other relevant baselines missing; e.g., federated dropout (which can be done on the weight level as well) is missing.
- The overall contribution seems incremental compared to the prior work of SWAT. Furthermore, the authors discuss how SWAT also employs activation sparsification but they seem to not use it at all in their experiments. 
- The overall state of the experimental evaluation is a bit weak; there is only one dataset considered where the number of clients is relatively small (100). What happens on datasets with more users for example on EMNIST / FEMNIST? Furthermore, it seems that the authors show results from single seeds and therefore it is hard to see what is the stability of their results.
- The authors don’t talk about the extra cost of transmitting the indices of the non-zero values in the main text (although they do mention the CSC format in the table caption), which can be an important additional communication cost in the case of unstructured pruning. Furthermore, at section 5 at the end, the authors mention $0.5(r_{mask }- sp)$ savings and it is not clear to me how is that derived and whether it does take into account sending the indices of the non-zero values as well.

As for other general feedback:
- At the first bullet before the related work, the authors argue that with the way their sparsity is realised  the privacy is better preserved. Could they perhaps elaborate more? To me it seems that this can actually be worse for privacy as each user can have its own sparsity pattern and thus reveal additional information compared to a server imposed sparsity pattern which is the same for all users.
- This work seems to be geared towards upload compression, although to me it seems that download compression is also feasible. Have the authors experimented with such an idea? 
- The algorithm needs some improvements, e.g., add an if statement for the different choices, as it can be confusing as it is now. Furthermore, the details Figure 3 are quite hard to see, so I would encourage the authors to use a different colour palette and intensity for the lines. 
- At the caption of table 1 the authors mention a 7.4x improvement over vanilla SWAT but the 7.4x seems to be on the dense model, i.e., when SWAT is not employed. Could the authors elaborate on what they compare against?
- Sometimes the authors refer to top-K and sometimes to top-K%. It would be better if they pick one for consistency.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, while ZeroFL is simple, it does seem to be a straightforward extension of SWAT to the federated setting with a very minor modification. The experimental evaluation is rather weak (experiments on a single dataset) and there is an absence of other critical baselines (such as federated dropout). These are the primary issues that lead me to recommending rejection.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Complex Locomotion Skill Learning via Differentiable Physics

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes to learn locomotion skills on soft robots made of springs. It proposes to leverage differentiable physics along with a NN controller based on SIREN, which allows to directly learn policies by minimizing loss functions defined on trajectories. The methods allows to learn locomotion and jump behaviors on a various set of 2D and 3D mass-spring systems.
reviews.main_review:
## Strengths

* The loss function design is clear and well explained, it is specifically tailored for cyclic motion which is the main task addressed by the paper.
* The use of SIREN to process the input and output a periodical signal is quite interesting. It suits well to tasks requiring cyclic motion.
* The comparison to PPO seems fair and thorough, I appreciate that the authors put efforts into having a well-tuned baseline, something which is often overlooked.
* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes to learn locomotion skills on soft robots made of springs. It proposes to leverage differentiable physics along with a NN controller based on SIREN, which allows to directly learn policies by minimizing loss functions defined on trajectories. The methods allows to learn locomotion and jump behaviors on a various set of 2D and 3D mass-spring systems.

Main Review:
## Strengths

* The loss function design is clear and well explained, it is specifically tailored for cyclic motion which is the main task addressed by the paper.
* The use of SIREN to process the input and output a periodical signal is quite interesting. It suits well to tasks requiring cyclic motion.
* The comparison to PPO seems fair and thorough, I appreciate that the authors put efforts into having a well-tuned baseline, something which is often overlooked.
* The control of complex mass spring system is impressive

## Weaknesses

* The claim: "To the best of our knowledge, we proposed the first differentiable physics based learning frame-
work for complex locomotion tasks using single network." is a bit fishy.
Many RL algorithms handle locomotion tasks of humanoid-like robots in simulation with a single neural network. I understand the current framework use differentiable physics for mass-spring systems, and thus is not directly applicable to classical RL benchmarks without a differentiable physics simulator. Such a claim could be made if it solved classical RL locomotion tasks + harder ones for example.

* The experiment section is not really convincing and hard to follow. 
** Table 1 is barely commented, there is only a bold number in one column. Only tanh activation function is compared to sin, while relu, gelu, sigmoid could be used for hidden layers with an activation function outputting a value in [-1, 1] for the final layer of the network. Given that using SIREN seems to be an important component, the paper would benefit from a better ablation study. 
** The importance of features section is really unclear. 
There are sentences like "The results show that each of the three parts serves as a necessary
component for a converged training. The periodic control signal serves as an important role. The
agent can hardly move without this signal" without reference to a table or a figure, so I do not know what to look to confirm the statement.
Figure 6 is also hard to read and I am not sure if the section is super insightful. To my understanding it validates the design choice of the input feature which is a bit minor compared to other components of the model.
* In the abstract, a modified Adam optimizer is mentioned "we find our adoption of batching and modified Adam optimizer effective in training complex locomotion tasks". It seems to be important according to the authors but there is no experiment on vanilla Adam vs the proposed one with reduced momentum so we cannot asses how useful it is.

## Other comments

* The abbreviations of Table 1 could be put as caption rather than footnote for readability. The back-and-forth between table and footnote is not great for the reader.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper is interesting, proposing a clean framework to perform locomotion tasks on mass-spring systems. The benefit of using differentiable physics is clearly highlighted and the use of SIREN to learn periodic motions is on point. However the experimental section is weak. For this reason I only grade it as weak-reject but would be inclined to bump it if this part is strengthened.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)

Summary  
This paper presents a framework for learning locomotion and jumping behaviors of soft robots by combining differentiable physics simulation with a SIREN-based neural controller. The authors introduce a cyclic motion–specific loss function, leverage sine-based activations to generate periodic control signals, and compare their approach against a well-tuned PPO baseline. Experimental results on complex mass-spring systems demonstrate the promise of the method. However, several aspects of the experimental evaluation require clarification and additional ablations before the contributions can be fully validated.

Strengths  
1. The loss function is carefully designed and clearly explained, specifically tailored to enforce cyclic motion in the learned behaviors.  
2. Employing a SIREN (sinusoidal representation) network to encode periodic control signals effectively matches the cyclic nature of locomotion tasks.  
3. The comparison to PPO is fair and thorough; the baseline is well tuned and provides a solid point of reference for performance evaluation.  
4. Demonstrating control of high-dimensional, complex mass-spring soft-robot systems is impressive and showcases the potential of differentiable physics for soft-body locomotion.

Weaknesses  
1. The claim that this is the first single-network, differentiable-physics framework for complex locomotion overlooks prior RL-based humanoid locomotion work and thus overstates novelty.  
2. The experimental section is difficult to follow and in some cases unconvincing:  
   – Table 1’s metrics use abbreviations that are only explained in a footnote, and the main text offers minimal commentary on the results.  
   – The activation-function ablation only compares sine versus tanh, omitting other common choices such as ReLU, GELU, and sigmoid.  
   – The “importance of features” analysis is presented without explicit references to figure or table numbers, making it hard to assess.  
   – Figure 6 (gradient distribution) is cluttered and its relevance to the paper’s contributions is unclear.  
3. While the paper introduces a “modified Adam” optimizer with reduced momentum, no experiments compare it against standard Adam to quantify its actual benefit.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Rephrase or qualify the novelty statement to acknowledge related differentiable-physics and RL approaches to locomotion, ensuring the contribution is accurately positioned.  
2. Extend the activation-function ablation study to include ReLU, GELU, and sigmoid in hidden layers, along with an appropriate bounded function for the output layer, to justify the choice of sine activations.  
3. When discussing feature-importance results or other ablations, explicitly cite the corresponding figure or table numbers in the text for clarity.  
4. Conduct a direct comparison between vanilla Adam and the proposed reduced-momentum Adam optimizer to demonstrate the actual impact of this modification.  
5. Move the abbreviations in Table 1 from the footnote into the table caption and expand the discussion in the text to guide the reader through the key takeaways.

Overall Assessment  
This work addresses an important problem—soft-robot locomotion via differentiable simulation—and introduces a clean formulation with a tailored loss and SIREN-based controller. The strong PPO baseline comparison and control of complex mass-spring systems are definite assets. However, the paper’s novelty claim needs qualification, and several experimental components (activation functions, optimizer choice, feature-importance analysis) require more comprehensive ablations and clearer presentation. With these revisions, the paper would make a solid contribution to the field. I therefore recommend a marginal acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.
reviews.main_review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper provides a practical guide on how to train an NN-based controller for locomotion tasks with robustness and efficiency. 

The tasks are categorized into motion primitives including running, jumping, and crawling, each with a manually designed objective function. Considering the periodic nature of locomotion, the author proposed to use sine activation layers and periodic signals in different phases as input. The experiments show that a larger batch size can lead to faster convergence. Compared to SGD, this paper points out that Adam is better for the tasks. The supplementary video presents that the trained networks enable real-time control of complex skills.

Main Review:
Based on existing differentiable physics engines, this paper further gives empirical recipes on how to use the physics gradients to train powerful controllers, which can help the community to develop techniques related to differentiable physics. 

This method divides long-horizon tasks into smaller periods with different objectives. To avoid sparse and non-smooth rewards, objective functions are manually designed for subtasks. Periodic control input is also added to the input to actuate the controller. When training the network, the authors use batching, regularization terms, and sine activation layer to accelerate and stabilize the convergence.

When reading this paper, I have several concerns,
1. In Equation 5, how is $g_v(n)$ subtracted from $A(t)$? Do they have the same dimension? $g_v(n)$ as a velocity should have a dimension of 3 (or 6 if rotation is included). The dim of  $A(t)$ should be the DOF of actuation. Maybe you mean $||g_v(n)||$. Why only target velocity is considered, how about the height $g_h(n)$? Why “periodic control signal may induce unwanted high-frequency noises”？
2. The paper mentions "a modified Adam optimizer". I did not find how Adam is modified.
3. In Figure 4, what is the definition of one iteration? Is it one step of network update or one step of simulation?

Summary Of The Review:
This paper gives us a good example of how to train a network using differentiable physics. Their video of interactively controlling 2D and 3D robots looks interesting. Although some of the techniques like staging, batching, since activation, and periodic signals are intuitive and existing tricks, it is good to see that a combination of them can successfully train a controller.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a practical and well‐engineered approach for training neural network locomotion controllers via differentiable physics. By decomposing long‐horizon tasks into periodic subtasks (running, jumping, crawling) with tailored objectives, introducing sine‐based control inputs and activations, and leveraging large batch sizes alongside a modified Adam optimizer, the authors achieve robust multi‐skill control and faster convergence compared to reinforcement learning baselines. Real‐time interactive demonstrations on both 2D and 3D soft‐body robots further underscore the method’s applicability.

Strengths  
- Provides a clear, empirically validated recipe for exploiting differentiable physics gradients in locomotion control.  
- Effectively partitions complex behaviors into shorter, periodic phases with custom loss terms for velocity and crawling.  
- Leverages periodic sine inputs and sine activation layers that naturally align with cyclic locomotion patterns.  
- Shows that increasing batch size and adapting the Adam optimizer yields faster, more stable convergence.  
- Includes supplementary videos demonstrating interactive control of 2D and 3D soft robots, highlighting real‐time performance and multi‐skill transitions.

Weaknesses and Technical Concerns  
1. Equation 5 Ambiguity  
   • It is unclear how the target velocity g_v(n), defined in task space (3D or 6D), is subtracted from the actuator‐space vector A(t). The dimensionality mismatch and appropriate norm operation are not specified.  
2. Omission of Height Target in Loss  
   • While the paper discusses height objectives g_h(n) in the text, the formal loss only incorporates g_v(n). The rationale for excluding g_h(n) or its implementation details are missing.  
3. Unsubstantiated Noise Claim  
   • The statement that periodic control signals can induce high‐frequency noise lacks theoretical analysis or citation. Readers would benefit from empirical evidence or references supporting this claim.  
4. “Modified Adam” Details  
   • The manuscript references a customized Adam optimizer that improves convergence, yet does not describe which hyperparameters (β₁, β₂, learning rate schedule) were altered or how.  
5. Undefined Iteration in Figure 4  
   • In the convergence plots, the term “iteration” is used without clarifying whether this refers to network weight updates or simulation steps, leaving the x‐axis interpretation ambiguous.

Suggestions for Improvement  
- Clarify Equation 5 by specifying how g_v(n) is mapped to actuator space or normalized before subtraction, and include the norm operation if used.  
- Explain the treatment of height targets g_h(n) in the loss function or justify their exclusion to prevent reader confusion.  
- Provide an analysis or literature reference demonstrating why periodic signals may introduce high‐frequency noise, or include an ablation study.  
- Detail the exact modifications to the Adam optimizer—e.g., adjusted momentum coefficients or learning rate schedule—to enable reproducibility.  
- Explicitly define what constitutes a single “iteration” in Figure 4 (e.g., one optimizer step, N simulation steps, or one epoch) to clarify the convergence timeline.

Recommendation  
This work makes a valuable contribution by assembling and validating a set of practical techniques for differentiable‐physics‐based locomotion control. Despite the aforementioned clarifications needed in several technical sections, the core ideas are sound, experimentally supported, and of interest to the robotics and learning communities. I recommend acceptance (rating: 6 – marginally above threshold), pending minor revisions to address the points above.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper a useful, practical guide on training neural locomotion controllers via differentiable physics, demonstrating robust multi‐skill control and faster convergence than RL. They appreciate the combination of known techniques (staging, batching, sine activations, periodic input) but raise questions about certain technical details.
strengths: Provides an empirical recipe for leveraging differentiable physics gradients to train powerful controllers
Breaks long‐horizon tasks into shorter periods with tailored objectives for running, jumping, and crawling
Introduces periodic control signals and sine activation layers to match locomotion periodicity
Demonstrates that larger batch sizes and a modified Adam optimizer accelerate and stabilize convergence
Supplementary video shows real‐time interactive control of both 2D and 3D soft robots
weaknesses: Equation 5: unclear how target velocity g_v(n) (a 3D or 6D vector) is subtracted from actuation A(t) (actuator‐space)
Loss design only considers target velocity g_v(n); omission of height target g_h(n) is not explained
Statement that periodic control signals may induce high‐frequency noise is not justified
The paper mentions a “modified Adam optimizer” but does not specify how Adam was changed
Figure 4: the meaning of one ‘iteration’ (simulation step vs. network update) is undefined
suggestions: Clarify the dimensionality and proper norm operation when subtracting g_v(n) from A(t) in Equation 5
Explain how the height target g_h(n) is incorporated (or justify its omission) in the loss formulation
Provide an analysis or citation on why periodic control signals introduce unwanted high‐frequency noise
Detail the specific modifications made to the standard Adam optimizer (e.g., changed β parameters)
Define explicitly what constitutes one training iteration in Figure 4 (number of simulation steps vs. weight update)
main_points: Paper offers a step‐by‐step guide for training NN‐based locomotion controllers using differentiable physics
Long‐horizon tasks are divided into smaller, periodic subtasks with custom objective functions
Incorporation of periodic sine inputs and sine activations aligns network control with cyclic motion
Batching, regularization terms, and optimizer choice (Adam) are key to fast, stable convergence
Demonstrated multi‐skill, interactive real‐time control in both 2D and 3D soft‐body simulations
technical_details: Equation 5: subtraction of g_v(n) from A(t) lacks dimension alignment and may require a norm
Manual design of velocity, height, and crawling objective functions to avoid sparse/non‐smooth rewards
Use of multi‐phase sine waves as periodic control signals input to the NN
Claim of a “modified Adam” optimizer—details (β1, β2, learning rate changes) are missing
Ambiguous definition of ‘iteration’ in the training pipeline diagram (Fig. 4)

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to use differentiable simulators for learning locomotion skills for deformable characters. The key contribution is the experiments and analysis of a large number of design choices, including activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc. The paper finds that by combining differentiable simulator with the right design choices, the character can learn general and complex skills with one policy. The paper also shows that this combination can significantly outperform PPO.

Main Review:
Strengths:
1) This paper combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, which enables effective learning of soft body locomotion.
2) The analysis and ablation study provides useful and practical guidance to practitioners in the field of reinforcement learning and locomotion control.

Weakness:
1) Technical contribution is lean. The Taichi differentiable simulator is an existing work. This paper seems to use it without lots of changes. The second claimed contribution "end-to-end differentiable simulation environment, including mass-spring and material point" is not well supported. The description of simulation is high level or in Section 2. It is hard to understand the scope of this contribution.

2) I hope that the paper can demonstrate more impressive and diverse results. For example, a soft character navigating uneven terrains, or a soft robot manipulating objects. A soft body character running on flat ground, as shown in this paper, has been demonstrated in several prior works, such as DiffTaichi. Even though this paper demonstrates multiple locomotion skills (turning, different speed and jumping) in one policy, the improvement over the prior work is incremental.

Additional questions and comments:
1) The paper claims in Section 2.1 that "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied to our learning framework". I thought that the simulator needs to be differentiable. So only a small subset of simulation will work. Did I miss anything?

2) It is unclear to me how applicable the results of the ablation study are to other tasks. For example, the sine actuation function and the periodic signal may not be as important for manipulation tasks. If the application of this paper is only locomotion, its contributions are quite limited.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper demonstrates good results for learning soft body locomotion. Although the technical novelty is lean, I find that the analysis and ablation of various design decisions are helpful, and has practical values to guide further development in this direction. For this reason, I am slightly leaning towards accepting the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper investigates learning soft-body locomotion policies by coupling a differentiable physics simulator with systematic design choices in network activation, reward shaping, optimizer selection, batch size, and control signals. Through extensive experiments and ablation studies, the authors demonstrate that a single, unified controller can learn diverse gaits for deformable characters and consistently outperform PPO baselines on flat-ground running tasks.  

Strengths  
1. Combination of Differentiable Simulation and Design Insights: By leveraging the Taichi differentiable simulator alongside carefully tuned components—such as sine versus ReLU activations, periodic input signals, and reward weighting—the work achieves robust soft-body locomotion with minimal trial-and-error.  
2. Thorough Ablation Studies: The paper presents a comprehensive analysis of how each design choice (activation functions, reward terms, optimizers, batch sizes, state encoding, etc.) impacts learning. These results offer concrete, actionable guidance for practitioners in reinforcement learning and soft-robotics control.  

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Technical Novelty: The core simulator is taken from existing Taichi infrastructure, and there are few substantive extensions or algorithmic innovations. As a result, the paper’s main contribution resides in engineering and empirical evaluation rather than in new methods.  
2. Unclear Scope of End-to-End Differentiable Environment: Although the authors claim support for both mass-spring and Material Point Method (MPM) simulations in an end-to-end differentiable stack, the manuscript only provides high-level descriptions (Section 2) without detailing interfaces, gradient flow, or computational trade-offs.  
3. Narrow Experimental Scenarios: All experiments focus on flat-ground running tasks. While the gains over PPO are consistent, it is difficult to assess generality or robustness in more challenging settings (e.g., uneven terrain, obstacles, or object manipulation).  
4. Overstated Generality Claim: The statement that “any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied” overlooks the critical requirement of differentiability. It remains unclear how easy it would be to port this approach to simulators that were not originally designed for gradient-based learning.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Broaden Experimental Tasks: Augment the evaluation suite with more challenging scenarios—such as locomotion over uneven terrain, jumping, turning, or soft-robot manipulation tasks—to better demonstrate the controller’s generality and robustness.  
2. Expand Technical Details on Differentiable Simulation: Provide a more granular description of the end-to-end pipeline, including how mass-spring and MPM modules are integrated, how gradients are computed and propagated, and any limitations in simulation scale or time-step selection.  
3. Clarify the Differentiability Requirement: Discuss the constraints and assumptions required by the simulator to remain fully differentiable, especially if one wishes to adopt alternative physics engines. This will strengthen the claim of broad applicability.  
4. Discuss Broader Applicability of Ablation Findings: Reflect on whether the observed benefits of sine activations or periodic control signals extend beyond locomotion to tasks such as soft-object manipulation, deformation tracking, or dynamic interaction with the environment.  

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper delivers valuable empirical insights into the role of design choices when training soft-body locomotion policies with differentiable physics. The combination of a unified controller and detailed ablation studies makes it a useful reference for researchers and practitioners. However, the technical novelty is modest, and several claims—particularly regarding end-to-end differentiability and generality—require clearer substantiation. Furthermore, expanding the experimental scope would greatly strengthen the argument that these methods scale to more complex tasks.  

Given its strong empirical contributions but limited methodological novelty, I recommend a rating of 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer appreciates the paper’s extensive experiments and ablation studies showing that carefully chosen design decisions (activation function, reward design, optimizer, batch size) combined with a differentiable simulator can enable one policy to learn complex soft-body locomotion skills and outperform PPO. However, they find the technical novelty limited and request clearer support for some claims and more diverse, challenging experiments.
strengths: Combines differentiable simulation with many carefully chosen design decisions, enabling effective soft-body locomotion learning.
Provides thorough analysis and ablation studies that offer practical guidance for reinforcement learning and locomotion control practitioners.
weaknesses: Technical contribution is limited: it largely reuses the existing Taichi differentiable simulator with few novel changes.
The claim of providing an end-to-end differentiable simulation environment (mass-spring and material point method) is not well supported; descriptions are high-level and unclear in scope.
Experimental scenarios are not diverse or sufficiently challenging (e.g., only flat-ground running), making the improvements over prior work incremental.
suggestions: Demonstrate more impressive and diverse results, such as soft characters navigating uneven terrain or soft robots manipulating objects.
Provide clearer and more detailed descriptions of the end-to-end differentiable simulation environment and the scope of that contribution.
Clarify the generality claim "Any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied" by explaining the differentiability requirement and its limitations.
Discuss how the ablation-study findings (e.g., importance of sine actuation and periodic signals) generalize to tasks beyond locomotion, such as manipulation.
main_points: Use of differentiable simulators to learn locomotion skills for deformable characters.
Extensive experiments and analysis of design choices (activation functions, reward design, optimizer, batch size, etc.).
Combining differentiable physics with proper design choices yields a unified controller capable of general and complex skills, outperforming PPO.
Technical novelty is lean, largely reusing existing differentiable simulation infrastructure.
The end-to-end simulation environment claim (mass-spring and MPM support) lacks detailed support.
Experiments focus on flat-ground locomotion; lacks more challenging scenarios and tasks.
The claimed generality to any deformable simulator and to other tasks (e.g., manipulation) is unclear.
technical_details: Relies on the existing Taichi differentiable simulator without major novel modifications.
Claims support for both mass-spring and material point method simulators, but descriptions are high-level in Section 2.
Statement that "any physically-based deformable object simulator can be applied" overlooks the need for differentiability.
Ablation study shows sine activation functions and periodic control signals are important for locomotion, but this may not hold for manipulation tasks.
Design choices analyzed include activation functions (sin, tanh, relu, gelu, sigmoid), reward design, optimizer (Adam vs. SGD), batch size, periodic signal, state vector, and target encoding.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.
reviews.main_review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents an approach for interactive control of soft robot locomotion using differentiable simulation. The authors demonstrate impressive results in steering soft robots toward multiple goals in real time. However, I find the core technical contributions to be fairly narrow in scope, and the experimental and presentation aspects require clarification. Overall, I recommend a marginal rejection (Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold).

Strengths  
• The paper achieves strong experimental results in controlling soft robot locomotion and dynamically switching among different goals.  
• The real-time interactive control demonstrated is compelling and shows promise for human-in-the-loop robot steering.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Technical Contribution  
   – The custom loss design is tailored specifically for locomotion objectives and it is unclear whether this formulation would generalize to other types of soft robots or tasks, or translate to real-world hardware experiments.  
2. Weak and Inconsistent Support for Sine Activation  
   – Table 1 reports only marginal performance gains of the sine activation over tanh. Appendix Table 5 even shows cases where tanh outperforms sine. While the authors note that tanh can saturate over long backpropagation sequences, the empirical advantage of sine remains unconvincing.  
   – No comparisons are provided against other popular nonlinearities such as ReLU, leaving open the question of whether sine offers any unique benefit.  
3. Presentation and Clarity Issues  
   – The so-called “modified Adam” optimizer is introduced without specifying which hyperparameters or update rules differ from the standard Adam algorithm. This makes it impossible to attribute any performance differences to the proposed changes.  
   – Robot agent names first appear in Figure 7 (Section 4.5), yet they are referenced earlier in Section 3 and Table 1. This late definition disrupts the reader’s understanding.  
   – At the end of Section 4.2, the text refers to Figure 5 for a comparison between SGD and Adam, but Figure 5 does not actually contain that comparison, indicating an incorrect figure reference.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Perform an ablation or comparative study including ReLU (and potentially other activations) to more convincingly demonstrate the advantages of the sine activation.  
• Clearly describe the modifications made to Adam (e.g., changes to learning rate schedules, moment estimates, or weight decay) and include a side-by-side comparison with the original Adam optimizer.  
• Introduce and define each robot agent’s name at first mention (e.g., in Section 3 or Table 1) rather than deferring until Figure 7.  
• Correct the faulty reference in Section 4.2 so that the stated SGD vs. Adam comparison points to the appropriate figure or table.

Conclusion  
The authors address an important problem—interactive soft robot locomotion control—and deliver strong qualitative results. Unfortunately, the novelty of the loss design appears limited to locomotion tasks, the support for the sine activation is inconclusive without broader comparisons, and several presentation issues hinder reproducibility and clarity. Given these concerns, I recommend a marginal rejection (Rating: 5).
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes to learn neural network controllers in a differentiable simulator for locomotion tasks. A set of technical improvements are made and make the controllers more robust and require fewer samplers to learn. These include: using periodic activation functions, design of loss functions, and using large batch size. The paper shows learned locomotion skills of running, jumping, and crawling for different soft robots, which can be interactively controlled.

Main Review:
### Strength
* +++ The paper shows great results of interactively controlling soft robot locomotion and switching among different goals.

### Weakness
* --- The overall technical contribution is limited. The proposed contribution on loss designs is limited to the locomotion tasks, and it is not clear whether these losses would generalize to other robots and the real world [1, 2].
* -- The use of the sine activation function is not well supported by the experiments. In table 1, sine function shows marginal improvement over tanh, and in table 5 of the appendix, using tanh shows much better performance over sine. Furthermore, tanh tends to become saturated during backpropagation of a long sequence. I would suggest the author also compares with ReLU.
* -- Presentation in the paper can be improved:
     1. The modified Adam optimizer is listed as one of the technical contributions. However, it is not stated clearly what is the modification and how does it compare to the original Adam optimizer. 
     2. The name of the robots are presented in Fig. 7 of Sec. 4.5 but are referenced multiple times in previous sections and table 1. It would be helpful to clarify the name of the agents early on. 
     3. Reference of Fig. 5 at the end of Sec. 4.2 seems to be incorrect, as no comparison of SGD and Adam is shown in Fig. 5.

[1] Lee, Joonho, et al. "Learning quadrupedal locomotion over challenging terrain." Science robotics 5.47 (2020).
[2] Zhao, Allan, et al. "RoboGrammar: graph grammar for terrain-optimized robot design." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG) 39.6 (2020): 1-16.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows interesting results of locomotion tasks, but the overall technical contribution is limited as presented. The writing of the paper could also be further improved. I am willing to raise my score if the listed concerns can be addressed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: On the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Feature Propagation in Learning on Graphs with Missing Node Features

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. reviews.main_review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Initial assessment : accept

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. Missing value imputation is a problem that is often ill-addressed when it comes to ML research. In this paper, the authors specifically look at imputation in the context of training GNNs, and use the graph to perform the imputation. 

I feel the authors need to address the difference between the proposed method and label propagation a bit more. They claim it's different because of labels being discrete and the diffusion operator being different, but I'm not clear why that's a deal breaker. Won't the algorithm be the same but with a new operator? 

Another point to address is the extension to vector valued features. They claim the extension is straightforward, but a note to this effect will be nice. 

other comments:

Fig 3: Please make the text larger. The figures are hard to read. 

Fig 3: Also any intuition why Random is so much worse than Zero in all these datasets?

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the case of training GNNs with missing features. The proposed framework consists of a diffusion based step prior to training the GNN. A discretization of the approach leads to what they term "feature propagation", a scalable method to impute features on the graph. The assumption is that the energy function that determines how related the features are to each other can be learnt from the data. The authors consider the gradient flow and it's solution to minimize the dirichlet energy, which is far more scalable than computing the closed form solution. Experiments on multiple datasets show that the proposed method outperforms several baselines.

Main Review:
Strengths:

The paper is well written, and easy to follow. The problem the authors address is interesting, and the solution provided is simple, yet elegant. The experimental results are thorough, both in terms of comparing performance and speed of the method. 

Weaknesses:

It's not completely clear how the proposed method extends to vector valued features. Is the process repeated independently for each feature? or is there some "interdependencies" between feature dimensions that can be made use of? How does that affect the computational complexity?

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of imputing missing node features in graph neural networks by introducing a diffusion-based pre-processing step, which the authors call feature propagation. The approach formulates imputation as minimizing the Dirichlet energy on the graph and implements an efficient discrete approximation via gradient flow, avoiding expensive matrix inversions. Extensive experiments on several benchmarks demonstrate that feature propagation consistently outperforms common imputation baselines in both predictive accuracy and runtime.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is well written and easy to follow, with a logical progression from the Dirichlet-energy formulation to the scalable iterative algorithm.  
2. Novelty and Relevance: Missing features in GNNs is an important but underexplored problem, and the proposed diffusion-based solution is both simple and elegant.  
3. Empirical Validation: The authors provide a thorough experimental evaluation, comparing multiple baselines (zero-fill, random-fill, and more) and reporting improvements in accuracy as well as significant speed gains.  
4. Theoretical Foundation: Framing imputation as a continuous diffusion minimizes a principled energy and offers insight into why the method works.

Weaknesses and Points for Clarification  
1. Vector-Valued Features: It remains unclear how feature propagation extends to multi-dimensional node features. Do you propagate each channel independently, or is there a joint diffusion scheme that captures interdependencies? How does this choice affect computational cost and convergence?  
2. Relation to Label Propagation: The paper notes that label propagation uses a different diffusion operator for discrete labels, but the distinction feels under-explained. Beyond treating labels as discrete versus features as continuous, what are the formal or practical differences in the underlying operators or convergence behaviors? Could these methods be unified under a single framework?  
3. Task-Agnostic versus Task-Aware Imputation: The authors suggest that task-agnostic imputation may be suboptimal but do not fully justify why a downstream-aware scheme would be preferable. A balanced discussion comparing the pros and cons of each paradigm would strengthen the narrative.  
4. Empirical Oddity in Baselines: It is surprising that random initialization consistently underperforms zero-fill. Some intuition or brief analysis (e.g., why GNNs can more easily ignore zero entries) would help the reader understand this phenomenon.  
5. Readability of Figures: In particular, Figure 3’s text annotations are small and somewhat difficult to read; increasing the font size would improve clarity.

Detailed Suggestions  
• In a revised version, please include a subsection on extending the algorithm to vector-valued features. Specify whether you run independent diffusion per feature dimension or jointly propagate multi-dimensional signals, and report on any computational or empirical trade-offs.  
• Augment Section 3 (or add an appendix) with a side-by-side comparison of your diffusion operator versus the classical label propagation operator, highlighting both theoretical and implementation differences.  
• Expand the discussion (perhaps in Section 5) on when task-agnostic imputation suffices versus when task-aware approaches could yield gains, citing existing literature on end-to-end imputation or joint learning.  
• Add a few sentences analyzing why random feature fill performs worse than zero. Even a qualitative argument about neural network inductive biases toward zeros would be valuable.  
• Adjust the plotting scripts so that all axis labels and legend text (especially in Figure 3) are legible at standard conference paper sizes.

Recommendation  
Overall, this paper makes a valuable contribution by providing a principled, efficient, and effective method for imputing missing node features in GNNs. Despite the above points for clarification, the core idea is solid, the experiments are comprehensive, and the results are compelling. I therefore recommend acceptance.  
Rating: 8: Accept, good paper.
